narrative:
As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in some positive ways, but the admissions model focused heavily on predictors of bar exam success. The model assessed your overall application and predicted a 92% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is the key benchmark for admission decisions—essentially, it flagged your candidacy as too high-risk for the program. This led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their legal careers.

The three biggest factors driving this prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school grade decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school grade decile—all of which pushed the model toward expecting failure rather than success. Most significantly, your placement in the bottom decile for Year 3 grades (compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile) signaled to the model a troubling dip in academic momentum, strongly contributing to the high failure odds. Your LSAT score of 26, well below the average of about 37 for admitted students, was the second-strongest pull toward failure, as it highlighted potential challenges in the analytical skills crucial for bar passage.

Even your Year 1 decile ranking at the bottom (versus the average around the 6th decile) added further weight in the same direction, suggesting early struggles that the model saw as persistent risks. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 is also below the typical 3.2, and other elements like your full-time status align with most applicants, they played smaller roles compared to these top academic indicators. In essence, your situation differs from typical successful applicants, who tend to show consistent mid-to-high decile performance across years and stronger entrance exam scores that buffer against failure predictions.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 35+ range, as even a 5-10 point gain could dramatically shift future model predictions. Strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help climb those decile rankings. Reach out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback, and explore bridge programs or alternative law schools that weigh holistic factors beyond these metrics—many students in your position rebound successfully with focused improvements.
